subject: ap-chemistry
title: Acid-Base Titration & Buffer Chemistry
stimulus:
Propanoic acid, CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH, is a carboxylic acid that reacts with water according to the equation above. At
25°C the pH of a 50.0 mL sample of 0.20 M CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH is 2.79.

parts.prompt:
Identify a Brønsted-Lowry conjugate acid-base pair in the reaction. Clearly label which is the acid and
which is the base.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept either pair: CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH (acid) / CH$_3$CH$_2$COO$^-$ (base), OR H$_3$O$^+$ (acid) / H$_2$O (base). Must label which is acid and which is base.

scoring_points.description: For identifying one correct conjugate acid-base pair with labels: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: CH3CH2COOH and CH3CH2COO− (acid / base) OR H3O+ and H2O (acid / base). 1 point is earned for writing (or naming) either of the Brønsted-Lowry conjugate acid-base pairs with a clear indication of which is the acid and which is the base.

sample_responses.response: $CH_3CH_2COOH$ (acid) and $CH_3CH_2COO^-$ (conjugate base) — they differ by one proton ($H^+$).
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the propanoic acid / propanoate ion pair and labels each member. The acid donates the proton; the conjugate base is the species after proton loss.

sample_responses.response: $CH_3CH_2COOH$ (acid) and $H_2O$ (base) — the acid donates a proton to water.
sample_responses.commentary: These two species are not a conjugate pair; they differ by more than one proton in the context of this equilibrium. A conjugate pair must differ by exactly one $H^+$.

required_elements: One of: CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH (acid) / CH$_3$CH$_2$COO$^-$ (base) OR H$_3$O$^+$ (acid) / H$_2$O (base).
alternatives.correct_example: CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH is the acid and CH$_3$CH$_2$COO$^-$ is its conjugate base — they differ by one proton. Alternatively, H$_2$O is the base and H$_3$O$^+$ is its conjugate acid.

wrong_examples: Pairing CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH with H$_2$O — those are not a conjugate pair (they differ by more than one proton).
common_traps: A conjugate acid-base pair differs by exactly one proton (H$^+$). The acid donates the proton to give the conjugate base.

parts.prompt: Determine the value of $K_a$ for propanoic acid at 25°C.

scoring_points.description: For the correct $K_a$ value with valid setup: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: [H3O+] = 10−pH = 10−2.79 = 1.6 × 10−3 M; Ka = [CH3CH2COO−][H3O+] / [CH3CH2COOH] = (1.6 × 10−3)2 / 0.20 = 1.3 × 10−5. 1 point each for solving [H3O+], writing/substituting the Ka expression, and the correct Ka value.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly converts pH to $[H_3O^+]$, uses the 1:1 stoichiometry for a monoprotic weak acid, applies the small-x approximation, and computes $K_a$.
pH is not a concentration. Must convert pH using $[H_3O^+] = 10^{-\text{pH}}$ before substituting into the $K_a$ expression.

wrong_examples: Using [H$_3$O$^+$] = 2.79 (reading the pH as a concentration directly).
Not squaring [H$_3$O$^+$] — the numerator is [H$_3$O$^+$][A$^-$], which equals [H$_3$O$^+$]$^2$ for a monoprotic acid.
common_traps: Convert pH to [H$^+$] using 10$^{-\text{pH}}$ before substituting into the $K_a$ expression.

parts.prompt:
For each of the following statements, determine whether the statement is true or false. In each case, explain
the reasoning that supports your answer.
(i) The pH of a solution prepared by mixing the 50.0 mL sample of 0.20 M CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH with a
50.0 mL sample of 0.20 M NaOH is 7.00.
(ii) If the pH of a hydrochloric acid solution is the same as the pH of a propanoic acid solution, then the
molar concentration of the hydrochloric acid solution must be less than the molar concentration of the
propanoic acid solution.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): False. Equal moles of acid and strong base → 100% conversion to propanoate salt. The propanoate ion (weak base) hydrolyzes → pH > 7. Part (c)(ii): True. HCl is a strong acid (fully dissociates); propanoic acid is weak (partially dissociates). For the same [H$^+$] / pH, the propanoic acid must have a higher initial concentration because only a fraction dissociates.

scoring_points.description: For 'false' with correct reasoning about the basic salt solution: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: False. The conjugate base of a weak acid undergoes hydrolysis at equivalence to form a solution with pH > 7. (CH3CH2COO− + H2O ⇌ CH3CH2COOH + OH−) 1 point is earned for noting that the statement is false AND providing a supporting explanation.

sample_responses.response: False. Mixing equal volumes of equal-concentration weak acid and strong base produces the conjugate base $CH_3CH_2COO^-$. This ion hydrolyzes in water ($CH_3CH_2COO^- + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CH_3CH_2COOH + OH^-$), producing $OH^-$ and giving a solution with pH > 7, not 7.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the equivalence point product as the conjugate base, writes the hydrolysis reaction, and concludes pH > 7.

sample_responses.response: True. At the equivalence point, all the acid has been neutralized, so the solution is neutral (pH = 7).
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. pH = 7 only occurs when a strong acid is neutralized by a strong base. The propanoate salt formed here is a weak base that hydrolyzes, raising pH above 7.

required_elements: The resulting solution contains sodium propanoate (the conjugate base of a weak acid), which is a basic salt — its hydrolysis produces OH$^-$, giving pH > 7.
alternatives.correct_example: False. At the equivalence point of a weak acid–strong base titration, the solution contains only the conjugate base (CH$_3$CH$_2$COO$^-$). This ion hydrolyzes: CH$_3$CH$_2$COO$^-$(aq) + H$_2$O(l) ⇌ CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH(aq) + OH$^-$(aq), producing OH$^-$ and making pH > 7.

wrong_examples: Saying true — neutralizing a weak acid with a strong base does not give pH 7; the resulting salt solution is basic.
common_traps: pH = 7 only when a strong acid is neutralized by a strong base. Weak acid + strong base → basic solution at equivalence point.

scoring_points.description: For 'true' with correct reasoning about strong vs. weak acid dissociation: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: True. HCl is a strong acid that ionizes completely. Fewer moles of HCl are needed to produce the same [H3O+] as the propanoic acid solution, which only partially ionizes. 1 point is earned for noting that the statement is true and providing a supporting explanation.

sample_responses.response: True. HCl is a strong acid and dissociates completely: $[HCl] = [H^+]$. Propanoic acid only partially dissociates. For both solutions to have the same pH (same $[H^+]$), the propanoic acid must have a higher initial concentration to generate the same number of $H^+$ ions through partial ionization.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly contrasts complete vs. partial dissociation and uses that to explain why propanoic acid must have a higher molarity to match the same pH.

sample_responses.response: False. A stronger acid at the same concentration would produce more $H^+$, so HCl would need a higher concentration for the same pH.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect reasoning. HCl produces more $H^+$ per mole, so it needs a *lower* concentration (not higher) to match the same pH as the partially-ionized propanoic acid.

required_elements: HCl is a strong acid (100% dissociation); propanoic acid is a weak acid (partial dissociation).
For the same pH (same [H$^+$]), propanoic acid must have a higher initial concentration.
alternatives.correct_example: True. HCl is a strong acid and dissociates completely: [HCl] = [H$^+$]. Propanoic acid is a weak acid and only partially dissociates, so [H$^+$] < [CH$_3$CH$_2$COOH]$_{initial}$. If both solutions have the same pH (same [H$^+$]), the propanoic acid concentration must be higher to produce that many H$^+$ ions through partial dissociation.

wrong_examples: Saying false — the HCl would actually have a LOWER concentration than the propanoic acid for the same pH because HCl is stronger.
common_traps: A strong acid at concentration $c$ produces [H$^+$] = $c$. A weak acid at the same $c$ produces [H$^+$] < $c$. So for equal [H$^+$], the weak acid needs a higher $c$.

parts.prompt: Calculate the molarity of the propanoic acid solution.
parts.scoring_notes: At equivalence point, moles acid = moles NaOH. $n$(NaOH) = (0.173 M)(0.02052 L) = 3.550 × 10$^{-3}$ mol. [acid] = 3.550 × 10$^{-3}$ mol / 0.02500 L = 0.142 M.

scoring_points.description: For the correct molarity with valid stoichiometric setup: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: n(NaOH) = (0.173 M)(0.02052 L) = 3.55 × 10−3 mol acid; M = 3.55 × 10−3 mol / 0.02500 L = 0.142 M. 1 point for moles of acid; 1 point for correct molarity.

sample_responses.response:
$M_A V_A = M_B V_B$
$M_A = \frac{(0.173\text{ M})(20.52\text{ mL})}{25.00\text{ mL}} = 0.142\text{ M}$
sample_responses.commentary: Applies the equivalence-point relation correctly for a monoprotic acid-strong base titration and calculates the correct molarity.

sample_responses.response: $M_A = \frac{(0.173)(0.02052)}{0.02500} = 0.142\text{ M}$ — but forgot to convert mL to L for the NaOH volume so got $\frac{(0.173)(20.52)}{0.02500} = 142\text{ M}$
sample_responses.commentary: Unit error: volume must be in liters (not mL) when calculating moles from molarity × volume. Always convert mL to L first.

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{NaOH}) = (0.173\text{ M})(0.02052\text{ L}) = 3.550\times10^{-3}\text{ mol}$
At equivalence: moles acid = moles NaOH = 3.550 × 10$^{-3}$ mol
$M = 3.550\times10^{-3}\text{ mol} / 0.02500\text{ L} = 0.142\text{ M}$

wrong_examples: Using mL instead of L in the molarity calculation.
Not recognizing that monoprotic acid reacts 1:1 with NaOH.
common_traps: Propanoic acid is monoprotic: 1 mol acid reacts with 1 mol NaOH at the equivalence point.

parts.prompt:
The student is asked to redesign the experiment to determine the concentration of a butanoic acid solution
instead of a propanoic acid solution. For butanoic acid the value of $pK_a$ is 4.83. The student claims that a
different indicator will be required to determine the equivalence point of the titration accurately. Based on
your response to part (b), do you agree with the student's claim? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Disagree. From part (b), pK$_a$ of propanoic acid ≈ −log(1.3 × 10$^{-5}$) ≈ 4.89. The pK$_a$ of butanoic acid is 4.83. Both are very close, so the pH at the equivalence point for both titrations would be similar (both slightly basic), and the same indicator would work for both.

scoring_points.description: For 'disagree' with reasoning that both pK$_a$ values are similar: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Disagree. From part (b), pKa for propanoic acid is −log(1.3 × 10−5) = 4.89. Because 4.83 is so close to 4.89, the pH at the equivalence point for butanoic acid should be close enough to use the original indicator. 1 point for disagreeing with a valid pKa / Ka / pH justification; 1 point for numerically comparing the two pKa values.

sample_responses.response: Disagree. From part (b), $pK_a(\text{propanoic}) = -\log(1.3\times10^{-5}) = 4.89$. Butanoic acid has $pK_a = 4.83$. These values are very close, so the equivalence point pH for both titrations will be nearly identical, and the same indicator will work for both.
sample_responses.commentary: Calculates the $pK_a$ of propanoic acid from part (b), numerically compares the two $pK_a$ values, and reaches the correct conclusion.

sample_responses.response: Agree. Butanoic acid has a different $K_a$ so the equivalence point will be at a different pH, requiring a different indicator.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect. Although the $K_a$ values differ slightly, the $pK_a$ values (4.83 vs. 4.89) are so close that the equivalence point pH is essentially the same. No change in indicator is needed.

required_elements: pK$_a$(propanoic) ≈ 4.89 and pK$_a$(butanoic) = 4.83 — these are nearly equal.
The equivalence point pH will be essentially the same for both titrations, so the same indicator works.
alternatives.correct_example: Disagree. From part (b), $K_a$(propanoic) ≈ 1.3 × 10$^{-5}$, so pK$_a$ ≈ 4.89. The pK$_a$ of butanoic acid is 4.83. Both values are very close, meaning the equivalence point pH will be similar for both titrations. The same indicator appropriate for one titration will also work for the other.

wrong_examples: Agreeing — the pK$_a$ values are nearly identical, so no indicator change is needed.
common_traps: The pH at the equivalence point of a weak acid titration depends on the pK$_a$ of the acid. Similar pK$_a$ → similar equivalence point pH → same indicator works.
